item.value: To build a web form that will collect information from the user
Created by Wimba Create, licenced to South Central College

image.alt: Forms: Getting information from the user

newpage.para: Forms allow you to start a conversation. Without forms you are only able to talk to the user, and they can only listen or read.

para.b: By adding forms to your web pages you have the ability to:

list.item: Collect data from your customers
Allow the user to make decisions and customize what feed-back they receive
Allow users to change the web site environment
A customer order form is an example of data collection.

newpage.para: Making several selections from drop-down boxes to determine which data the customer wants to view shows how forms can determine what information the customers should see. CarSoup.comhttp://carsoup.com/ is an excellent example of this use of forms.
Adding a single-button form to a page will allow users to change their environment, from frames to no frames, a different background color, turning music on and off, watching a video, etc.

image.alt: Several client computers sending requests to a server.

para.b: Before you learn the elements that make up forms and their objects let's go over the client/server model.

newpage.para: Remember that web pages "live" on a web server. A request is made from a client machine requesting a certain page from the web server. When the server receives the request it sends the necessary files back to the client and the web page displays in the browser window.
There are all types of files on the web server. Not only the HTML files (plain text) but image files as well. There are also small programs on the server called server-side scripts.
Just as the web page knows which image files should be displayed, the <form> element knows the name of the server-side script that should run when the form is submitted by the user.

para.b: When the user clicks on the "submit" button, the information from the form is sent to the server along with the name of the program, or script, that will handle the data.

newpage.para: A server-side script can do all types of things. A few examples are:

list.item: store the data in a database,
send an email to a specific address,
pull new data from a database (based on the form data criteria)
create a new web page

newpage.para: In this course you will only be learning how to create a form. You won't be able to manipulate data on the server because you won't have the ability to create the custom server-side scripts. Those techniques will be covered in courses pertaining to JavaScript, PHP and other scripting languages or server-side programming.
newpage.heading: Client-Server Architecture

paneltext.para: The architecture therefore involves two programs, a client 'browser'' and a server, communicating across a network... The server is free to generate the documents either by sending real files, or by generating virtual hypertext on the fly in response to a request.
- Tim Berners-Lee, et al.; World-Wide Web: An Information Infrastructure for High­Energy Physics; January 1992

newpage.pagetitle: Having a Conversation with a Web Server

image.alt: Information flowing from the client page to the server.

newpage.para: Client: Hello, could you give me a web page named orderBlank.html?
Server: Certainly, here it is along with three GIF files showing you the product as well as some cool buttons.

Client: This is great. I'm going to fill in all the blanks with my name, address, phone, and email and click on the submit button. (psst, when you get all my information please run the script named order.php.

Server: Okee Dokee. Let's see (rummaging through a shoebox filled with CGI scripts and PHP pages.) Ah, here it is, order.php. I'll just feed all your information into the script and see what happens.....

Ok, first the script saved all your order information in a database.
Then it built a new web page for you.
It looks like a verification page of your order.
Here you go. No graphics with this web page. Maybe next time.
Client: Oh good, just what I wanted. I'm going to print this out so I have a hard copy for my files. I'm off to look somewhere else on the web.

(Notice the client doesn't bother to respond to the server. The server is busy handling other requests and doesn't require a response from the client. It considered its job done when it responded with the verification.)

Note: The graphic shows CGI scripts. CGI stands for Common Gateway Interface. A CGI script is the interface between the client and the server or gateway. These are commonly written using Perl. Other options on the server include web pages with PHP code embedded in with the XHTML elements.
Server-Side Scripting

image.alt: List of server-side languages that are available.

newpage.para:
Server-side scripting allows the programmer to handle information from the client and run programs that will create new web pages on the fly. The benefit of this is that the programmer maintains control and the code is more secure.
Client-side languages, such as JavaScript respond differently on different browsers (you never know what your user will be viewing the web pages with) and the source-code is very visible to anyone that can see the web page.

para.b: Some of the more common server-side scripting languages include:

list.item: PHP - A very popular as a scripting language.
ASP.NET - Active Server Pages running on Microsoft servers.
JSP - Java Server Pages utilize the Java Language (the big brother to JavaScript)
Perl - one of the original languages used to create CGI scripts..
ColdFusion by Adobe is similar to PHP and ASP.NET and requires a special ColdFusion server.

para.b: All of these languages run on the web server and handle the form data as it is submitted by the user.

newpage.para: But enough of theory, let's start making a form!

The <form> element encloses all of the objects such as textboxes, buttons, and drop-down boxes. A form can include dozens of objects or just one. CSS and even tables can be used to help organize the form objects on a page.

para.b: The <form> tag is used within the <body> element of a web page.

newpage.para: Let's build a form containing all of the objects available to you as a web programmer

Create a simple web page with a table with 8 rows and 2 columns. Don't forget to add the summary attribute to the table as well as the comment block at the top of the page and descriptive comments for each form object.

Enclose the and
tags inside the
and
tags. Name the form "demo" using the name=" " attribute.

para.b: Here's what your code should look like:

image.alt: Code for form object

newpage.pagetitle: Objects to Put in the Form

image.alt: List of form objects and the recommended prefix for each.

para.b: There are 9 different controls that can be used inside the <form> element.

newpage.para: The Button, Reset, and Submit all make buttons and allow the web page to capture clicks from the user. The Button is a general purpose control or object while the Reset button clear's the values from all of the form objects. The Submit button's only purpose is to send the data in the form to the server.
Checkboxes are used to find user preferences when there can be more than one choice.
Radio buttons are used when there can only be one choice. They are named for the old fashioned car radios that allowed a person to select preset stations by pushing a row of buttons.
List boxes or drop-down boxes allow the user to select a choice (or several choices from a list). An example would be a list of state abbreviations.

Text, Password, and TextArea allow the user to type in text information. Text box is usually for short answers such as name, address, and email. Password text boxes display the input as a series of asterisks (*) to help keep passwords secure from on-lookers. Textarea allows the user to type in multiple lines of information. A comment box would be a good use for a textarea object.

The Hidden object is used to send information to the server that the end-user will not see. For example, sending the name of a web site that the server can use when generating an email. The web site is included in the subject line so the administrator or client can see where the email came from.

Each of these has a prefix that makes it easier to identify what it is, especially when you are writing code that runs out on the server. It makes a lot more sense knowing that you are working with a txtName text box than "name" which could be a text area, a radio button, or a drop down list.

para.b: Use the prefixes consistently with all your forms and your programming efforts will be much simpler in the future.
Let's add a text box to your form. Here is the code:

image.alt: Set up a text box using default values for faster development

para.b: Here are the results:

image.alt: Output of the textbox code.

newpage.para: The text box allows the user to type in single line entries. This is perfect for name, address, and phone numbers.

para.b: Here are the attributes you can use to customize this object:

newpage.para: type="text" tell the browser that this is a text box.

name=" " allows you to name the object so it can be referred to by the CGI script. (You should use the name attribute for every object inside a form.) Keep in mind that spaces are not allowed. Be smart and keep your names all lower case except for word boundaries and don't use plural names.
size=" " tells the browser the length of the text box in characters.
maxlength=" " tells the browser the maximum number of characters you will allow. If the maxlength is longer than the size, then the textbox will scroll as the user types in characters.

value=" " will assign a string value to the text box. This helps non-visual browsers but is an irritation for visual users because they have to delete the text before they can type their own text in. If you use value then keep it short.

Notice how the attributes can be stacked for readability. Remember, web browsers ignore the line returns in the code unless they find a
or a

tag.

Also, the value=" " attribute is used to supply a default value in the textbox. This is a real time-saver when programming with forms. All the programmer has to do is hit Enter for each form object and he or she doesn't waste time typing in information every time they run the page. Later, when the web page goes "live" this default value will be removed.

image.alt: Adding a password box to the file

newpage.para: The password text box is used to prevent others from seeing a password as it is typed in. It acts just like a textbox except that the characters display as shapes so they are unrecognizable.

Keep in mind that this is a display mechanism only. When the password is sent to the server across the web it is sent as normal text and can easily be extracted using a packet sniffer. If you are sending critical information such as passwords from your web page forms, make certain that the data is encrypted. This will be done by a server-side language and special Web connections such as a Virtual Private Network (VPN).

para.b: Add a password text box to the demonstration form.

newpage.para: Use the same <input /> element, but make the value of the type attribute "password":

image.alt: Code used to create a password input box.

newpage.para: Again, we used the value to set up a default value to save time during program development.

para.b: Here is what the output should look like:

image.alt: Output showing the textbox and password box.

para.b: Selection or drop down lists can allow the user to select an item from a list.

newpage.para: Unlike the elements we've used so far, this is a two-tag element that surrounds each of the <option> that should be included in the list.

size=" " The number of items displayed is determined by the size attribute. A value of "1" will make the list appear as a drop-down list and can be very useful when you don't want to waste real estate on the screen.

multiple="multiple" If you want the user to be able to select several options, add the attribute multiple to the <select> element. The user can select the items by holding the control key down and clicking each item to toggle it on or off.
selected="selected" can be used in the <option> that should be selected by default with the form is first displayed.
Add the following code. Don't forget, the <select> element will only work if it is between the <form> and </form> tags.)

image.alt: Code to add a list box to the form

para.b: Here is what the results should look like:

image.alt: Output showing the list box

newpage.para: Radio Buttons allow a single choice to be made from a list of choices. (If multiple options are wanted, then use checkboxes.)

image.alt: Code to add radio buttons to a form

para.b: And here is what the results will look like:

image.alt: Form with radio buttons added.

newpage.para: The name attribute is critical for radio buttons. Notice that it is the same for each radio button object. The browser uses the name to determine which radio buttons should be grouped together. With that information the browser automatically turns off radio buttons as others are selected by the user.

checked="checked" Add the attribute checked="checked" as part of the <input> element if you want a specific radio checked when the form is first displayed. If you don't include a checked attribute in one of the elements, none of them will be selected.

There's one other tricky piece about radio buttons. The value of the value attribute is what is sent to the server. Don't be fooled thinking the text after the <input> tag is sent, that is only for the end-user.
pic016.jpgUse the selection list or drop down box when you have more than three items to choose from. Selected lists also keep your forms from becoming too cluttered.
With the multiple attribute you can allow the user to select multiple entries.

Contingent (next to each other) entries can be selected by clicking on one item, holding down the shift key, and then clicking on the last item wanted in the list. All of the items in between the two will be selected.
If the CTRL key is held down, only the items clicked on will be selected. This works only if the multiple attribute is included in the <selection> element.

para.b: Use Radio Buttons when you have a short list (3 or fewer items) and you have the real estate to spare.
Checkboxes allow multiple options to be made by the user.
Here is the code you can add to your form:

image.alt: code for check boxes

para.b: Here is what the output should look like:

image.alt: Output showing checkboxes

para.b: Here are some of the attributes you can use:

newpage.para: type="checkbox" designates the input to be a checkbox.
name=" " The name attribute is used by the browser to determine which checkboxes are in the same group. This is very similar to the radio button object.
value=" " Like the radio button, the value is only sent to the server if the selection is checked.
checked="checked" If you want the box to be selected or checked when the page first displays, add the attribute checked="checked to the <input> element.

image.alt: Using the text area object in a form

newpage.para: The textarea box allows the user to input multiple lines of text. It is perfect for the typical comment box.

rows=" "
cols=" " The attributes for rows and cols allow you to determine how many lines of visible text will display on the page. If the user wants to type in more lines, the textarea will automatically scroll.
If you want text to display inside the textarea, add it after the start tag <textarea>. Keep it short though, because the user will have to delete your text before typing their own.
This is a two-tag element, so don't forget the ending tag </textarea>!
Add a textarea without any text showing to your demonstration page. Try it first with 100 cols to see how the width affects the table columns. Then change it back to 50 cols:

image.alt: code for textarea

newpage.para: Normally the <textarea> and </textarea> tags are put together without any space or lines between them.
If you put them on separate lines the browser will add spaces inside the text area and the cursor will sit in the middle.

para.b: Here is the output:

image.alt: Output using the Textarea box

newpage.para: Hidden fields are often used to send data to the server that the user doesn't know or care about. For example, a contact form on different websites sends information to the server which than sends out emails to specific people with the user's questions. How can the emails be automatically set up to show which site the email question originated from?
By using a hidden field this information can be sent to the server and included in the subject line and content of all the emails sent out.

Here's an example of an email from the Computer Careers server showing how the information from a hidden field is used in the Subject: line (personal information has been deleted for privacy purposes):

image.alt: An email showing how the hidden field is inserted into the subject line

newpage.para: Hidden fields can be placed anywhere within the <form> element although they are usually placed together in one location.

para.b: Add a hidden field to the demonstration form:

image.alt: Adding a hidden field to the form

newpage.para: Because it is hidden it will not appear on the web page form.
There are three types of buttons, submit, reset, and the plain ordinary old button. Which one is determined by the attribute type=" " which can have values of "submit", "reset", or "button".
type="submit" The submit button collects the information from the form and sends it to the server.

type="reset" The reset button clears all the input from the form and returns the objects to their default values if there are any. This is being used less and less because most often users simply go to the field that needs editing and make the fix. They rarely (if ever) want to start over and retype everything.

type="button" The button button can be used to get an action from the user and is usually connected with a JavaScript function. One example would be a button that would turn off the music on a web page. As soon as the user clicked the button, a short JavaScript function runs that toggles the sound off or on.
value=" " The value attribute will display on the face of the button.

para.b: Add a submit button to your demonstration form:

image.alt: Adding a submit button to the form

para.b: Here's what the finished form will look like:

image.alt: Final version of the form showing all the objects

newpage.para: The submit button now brings us back full circle: to the <form> tag. When the submit button is clicked, it uses the action=" " attribute and method=" " attribute in the <form> tag to determine how the form values should be sent to the server and what file should handle the form information out on the server.
, and how the data from the form should be transmitted to the server.
action=" " The action attribute is the name of the file on the web server which will read and handle the form values.

action=mailto:yourClientEmail@gmail.com You can also use the mailto: protocol to simulate using a script. When a browser sees mailto: it automatically opens up the email client program that is installed on the user's computer. (This only works when an email client is installed and will not work on computers, such as the school labs, that do not have a mail program installed.)
method=" " The method attribute tells the browser how to send the form values to the server. The values for this attribute can be either "post" or "get".

method="post" Post has the browser pack up the form values into a bundle, much like packing a suitcase full of data and shipping it over the Internet to the server. This "suitcase" of information can be encrypted if necessary.

method="get" Get takes the form values and adds them to the end of the URL request that is sent to the server. Using the get method is not secure and any information from the form can be easily seen by anyone that cares to capture the information. The is also a character limit to the get method.
So, let's do something with the form values by adding the necessary attributes to the <form> tag. Use your own email address to test everything out.

image.alt: Adding the necessary form attributes actionand method to the form element

para.b: When you click the submit button your email program will run.

newpage.para: And, because we use the method get you can see the values on the URL being sent to the server (in this case the mailto: email program)

image.alt: Showing the URL with the form values embedded after the URL address being requested.

newpage.para: enctype="text/plain" The enctype=" " attribute ensures that the data will be in a readable (line by line) format in the email that is sent.

If the enctype=" " is not used the data will be sent in the email as a clump of text. All the spaces are replaced with plus signs "+" and each variable is separated from the others with an ampersand "&".

para.b: If you use the attribute enctype="text/plain" each form value pair will be on a separate, easy-to-read line.
Update the subject line on the email by using this hack

newpage.para: You can change the subject line by adding it after a ? on the action.

para.b: Change the <form> attributes to look like this:

image.alt: Adding the enctype attribute to the form tag

para.b: When the submit button is sent an email is created. If you use the correct attributes you should see an email similar to this:

image.alt: form values being sent as an email.

para.b: When testing programs you will spend a lot of time testing something over and over.

newpage.para: It is very common for a developer to test a page 20 - 30 times getting everything just right. If that developer takes 60 seconds to input the test data each time, they will end up wasting over 20 - 30 minutes just typing in data!
When you are working on a tight deadline or trying to finish up a project in the small hours of the morning, 20 minutes is a lot of time!

para.b: Here's some tips that will save time and make your debugging easier:

list.item: Set up default values in the form when testing out the web page.

Keep things short. Instead of typing in a full name "John Jacob Jingle Himmer Schmidt", just type in the letter "a" or "first".
For multiple lines use "programming code" by hitting keys at random. Make sure to include lots of spaces so the line will word wrap: j klfjdj alksjfdj dfl jadflja dfk j ljdfja fjfljsalfjsfdj jf jadfl jlsdjf lsjfl jf fjlfdjlajfl lfdj ljj'jf lkj flj f.

Use smart data. Input data that matches the field.
A zip field might be "1111"
A state could be "MN".
A first name field might be "first".
Phone numbers can be (123) 456-7890.
Long numbers like serial numbers can be handled the same way: 1234567890123.

Keep track of which test is which. If you have to test something 10-20 times (not uncommon) it will be easier to track the results if you add it to your data. First name could be "name1", "name2", and "name3" for each consecutive trial.

Identify test data. Sometimes test data gets mixed up with live data. Always include phrases like "testing" or "testTextAreaj lkjd fljfljdj ldj fjdfl jkljflj etc." or "testStreet", "testCity", "testZip". If the data does get mixed in with real data it is easy to search and destroy.
Be consistent with your test data. Sometimes it is the data that breaks a program. Always use the same test data over and over so you know you are testing the form or program and not the data.

If things are not changing on the web page make sure you are viewing the correct file. Often you may end up editing one file and the browser is viewing another file. (I won't tell you how often this has happened to me, and I'm a paid professional.) In an very obvious spot, like at the end of an <h1> text add something weird like "ABC" and then view the page. If your new text does not display than check to make sure you are viewing the same file that you are editing.

newpage.heading: The faster and more consistent you can input test data, the less time it will take to debug your program or form.

image.alt: Set the tab order

newpage.para: American and European users expect to TAB through a form from left to right, top to bottom. Often if you add a new object in the middle of your form it will be in the wrong tab order.
tabindex=" " Add the tabindex=" " attribute to each of the controls on the form. This will give you the control you need to decide which field becomes active when the user hits the TAB key.
For example: <input name="fname" tabindex="1" />
accesskey=" " is another attribute that is helpful. It can be included with the following controls:

list.item: button
input
label
textarea.

newpage.para: In Windows the user holds down the ALT key and the specific accesskey to make the cursor jump to that control. (On the Macintosh, use the CTRL key.)
Don't forget to show the underlined character to let the user know that an access key (or hotkey) is available.

para.b: Here is the code showing you how to do this:

image.alt: Using accesskey to add a hot key to some of the text fields

newpage.para: Here is what the form will look like with the hot keys underlined to visually show what they are to the user:

image.alt: the altkey attribute in action

newpage.para: Use ALT n to jump to the name text box and use ALT p to jump to the password text box.

Notice how the default text is highlighted when the access key "ALT n" is used for the first name field. When text is highlighted, the next key hit automatically deletes the highlighted text and displays the incoming text. This is a real time saver for entering information into forms.
That wraps up forms. You will be using them more and more, especially when you start working with scripting languages such as PHP, ASP.NET, Java, C#, and VB.NET.

para.b: The demonstration form you created with this tutorial has all the essential code you need to create a form on your web page. Keep it as a quick reference for future classes (especially Web Programming!) as well as projects with your clients.

newpage.para: Here is a copy of the completed form web pagemedia/form.html.
This completes this tutorial: Web Forms, An Overview by Peter K. Johnson - April 2009
Read my blog at: http://webexplorations.com/bloghttp://webexplorations.com/blog

htmlinclude.center: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.5 License.
